title: Template — open-question
description: Fillable scaffold for a new open-question artifact (an unresolved decision to settle).
body:
**Why this matters.** _What breaks, or what job is blocked, if this stays unsettled — one sentence a newcomer (or an agent without the repo loaded) understands, before any identifier or ADR number._

## The question

_The decision to settle, in one sentence._

## Context

_Why it is open now — the forces and constraints, and what is blocked until it lands. Gloss every internal term, code identifier, and ADR number on first use._

## Diagram

_A picture when the subject is a structure, flow, or state machine — an ASCII box/flow diagram in a fenced code block. Omit for a pure value/policy choice._

## Options

_The candidate answers, each with its trade-off (name both sides — A vs B)._

## Recommendation

_The proposed answer and why — explicitly non-binding until the owner decides._

title: lifecycle status
description: A unit's status, drawn from six states — proposed, building, healthy, unhealthy, mapped, retired — that track it from authoring to proven to pruned.
body:
**In one line.** A unit's status, drawn from six states — proposed, building, healthy, unhealthy, mapped, retired — that track it from authoring through proven to pruned.

## What it is

Status lives on every tier (story / capability / contract) and is drawn from six states:

- **proposed** — authored but not yet selected for implementation; the initial state.

- **building** — selected and under active implementation (v1: `under_construction`); written at pickup as the first commit, before any code edits.

- **healthy** — proven: the unit reached `healthy` through its tier's proof mode at HEAD — a story by a UAT pass over fresh green capabilities, a capability by integration tests over fresh green contracts, a contract by its isolated unit test (or, where neither honest test exists, operator-attested) (ADR-0010).

- **unhealthy** — a once-healthy capability that has drifted (a contract test now fails, owned files changed, or the proof no longer matches HEAD); **computed** from evidence, never written to disk.

- **mapped** — brownfield: the capability is *observationally* verified by an existing target-repo test suite, without storytree driving a red→green flow; a distinct, weaker state than `healthy` — observational green never short-circuits to proven; v2 **supports** brownfield, the exact mapping mechanism under the owned loop / DBOS is still to design (see `open-questions.md` §2).

- **retired** — terminal off-tree state: pruned from the active tree; may carry `retired_reason` (prose) and `superseded_by` (an edge to its replacement).

## What it is not

`healthy` is not `mapped` (observational green never short-circuits to proven) and not `unhealthy` (a once-healthy unit that has since drifted). `mapped` and `unhealthy` are computed from evidence, never written to disk.
provenance: The brownfield `mapped` mechanism is open (`open-questions.md` §2).

title: unit fields
description: The four core fields a unit carries — outcome, guidance, title, id — covering its value statement, rebuild context, human label, and unique identifier.
body:
**In one line.** The four core fields a unit carries — outcome, guidance, title, id — covering its value statement, rebuild context, human label, and unique identifier.

## What it is

The four core fields a unit carries:

- **outcome** — a capability's plain-English, single-sentence value statement, with no conjunctions (if it needs them, split the unit instead).

- **guidance** — non-obvious technical context needed to rebuild a unit; only what an agent could not derive from outcome + proof.

- **title** — a short human label for a unit; not load-bearing for proof.

- **id** — a unit's unique identifier. v2 must allocate these **conflict-free across concurrent sessions** — a stated goal. An earlier DBOS spike showed durable, collision-free workflow IDs as one candidate mechanism, but DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so that mechanism is reference, not the adopted id-allocation path.

title: Cloud SQL access · Node connector · keyless IAM
description: How storytree reaches its Postgres runtime store: the Cloud SQL Node connector with IAM database auth via ambient ADC. Agent sessions need no per-session OAuth and no key files.
body:
**The choice.** Reach the Cloud SQL Postgres via the Node connector with keyless IAM database authentication — agent sessions authenticate from ambient Application Default Credentials, never a per-session OAuth or a key file.

## What it is

The `@google-cloud/cloud-sql-connector` Node connector (paired with `pg`, in `packages/store`) mints short-lived IAM tokens as the database password over a TLS socket — no password is stored. Identity comes from the operator’s Application Default Credentials already established on the dev host (plus a dedicated service account for headless/impersonated runs). The IAM database user is the operator email (`STORYTREE_DB_USER`), the database is `storytree`, and the instance connection name is `storytree-498613:australia-southeast1:storytree-pg`.

## Why this

No secrets in the repo and no per-session interactive login, so agents can run migrations and queries headlessly; it matches the stop/start cost posture (ADR-0015 §5) and keeps the browser/credential-free boundary (the studio never holds DB creds). A session should ASSUME keyless credentials are present and verify with `gcloud auth application-default print-access-token`, not assume it is unauthenticated.

## Constraints

The instance is STOPPED by default and bills only while up — burst with `pnpm db:up` → work → `pnpm db:down`. Auto-stop is **idle-aware** (ADR-0015 §5, `infra/idle-stop.tf`): a Cloud Function stops the instance only after ~60 min with zero DB connections, so it never stops a live session (a connection held by a session / the Auth Proxy resets the idle timer); a once-a-day blunt cron is the cost floor behind it. So a long idle gap will stop the DB — re-`db:up` if a query fails to connect. IAM users are bare roles, so a ONE-TIME privilege bootstrap (`infra/bootstrap-grants.sql`, applied as the throwaway-password `postgres` user via `packages/store/scripts/apply-grants.mjs`) granted the IAM user `CREATE` on the database; this is idempotent and already applied. The connector deps live in `packages/store`; DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so this is a plain typed Postgres connection.
provenance: Validated 2026-06-08: 73 units migrated keyless, then the instance stopped.

title: How should the studio reconcile structured docs vs rendered edits?
description: The DB stores structured Knowledge docs, but the studio writes rendered GuidanceAssets, so editing a unit in the studio converts it to rendered form (one-way). Decide how structure survives editing.
body:
**Why this matters.** Edit a Library entry in the studio today and it silently loses its structured fields — and because the shared store is now the live source of truth (ADR-0023), that loss lands in the authoritative copy, not a throwaway view. It is the master record itself being overwritten with a freeform note that strips the field labels, with no clean way to rebuild it.

## The question

The store holds structured Knowledge docs (per-kind fields) and is now the live source of truth (ADR-0023), but the studio writes rendered GuidanceAssets — so editing a unit in the studio collapses it to a rendered markdown body (one-way), degrading the authoritative copy. How should structure survive editing?

## Context

The studio's save path (the `pg` write) stores each entry as a single rendered-markdown `body`; the per-field structure (oneLine / whatItIs / options / …) is rebuilt on read by `renderBody()` and CANNOT be recovered from the rendered body without parsing it back. **ADR-0023 makes the shared store the live source of truth**, with `knowledge.json` only a **seed** and **no DB→seed export path yet** — so a studio edit now degrades the authoritative record itself, and the old escape hatch (re-seed structure from `knowledge.json`) both reverts any other live CLI edits and is explicitly warned against (ADR-0023: do not run `load-corpus` against a live DB that has CLI edits — it reverts them). ADR-0013 (structure-as-source) and ADR-0017 (templates derive from the schema) both want structure preserved; rendered-on-edit fights them, and now there is no clean rebuild.

## Diagram

```
  studio EDIT --> stores a RENDERED body in the store
                  (per-kind structure dropped, one-way)
                        |
   store = LIVE SOURCE OF TRUTH (ADR-0023);  knowledge.json = seed only
                        |
   re-seed from knowledge.json --> reverts ALL live CLI edits (ADR-0023 warns)
                        |
   => no clean path back to structure once an entry is edited in the studio
```
`renderBody()` = the function that rebuilds the field view on read; there is no inverse (rendered -> structured) and no DB→seed export yet.

## Options

- **A — Accept rendered-on-edit + re-seed**: let studio edits store rendered bodies and re-seed structure from `knowledge.json` on demand. Weakest now that ADR-0023 makes the store authoritative: re-seeding reverts live CLI edits and there is no DB→seed export, so the structured source rots while the store drifts to rendered-only.

- **B — Parse markdown back to structured on write**: the studio write reparses the rendered body into per-kind fields (inverse of `renderBody`) and stores a structured doc. Keeps one structured source of truth; but the parser is fragile and must round-trip every kind exactly.

- **C — Edit structured fields directly**: the studio edits the per-kind fields (not a freeform body), so writes are always structured. Truest to ADR-0013/0017/0023; largest client change (the wire shape the React client sees must carry fields, not just `body`).

## Recommendation

Non-binding: with ADR-0023 making the store authoritative and no DB→seed export, option A (re-seed) is now a weak stopgap — it reverts live edits and lets the structured source rot. Lean toward **C** (edit structured fields directly) as the durable fix, or **B** (parse-back) if the client wire shape must stay `body`-only. The owner decides.

title: Should STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE default to json or pg?
description: The studio backend is env-toggled between the offline `json` store and the live Cloud SQL `pg` store. Decide the default the toggle ships with.
body:
**Why this matters.** When you start the studio, should it default to your own private copy of the data — works offline and costs nothing, but every session drifts apart — or the shared company database, where everyone sees the same thing but it must be switched on first and bills while running? `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` is that switch, and we need to pick its default.

## The question

Should `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` default to `json` (offline-safe — the DB stays stopped, no cost, per-worktree state) or `pg` (shared live state across sessions — requires `pnpm db:up` and bills while up)?

## Context

The studio backend is being made switchable so the same `/api/*` contract serves either the offline `data/*.json` files or the live Postgres store. `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` is that switch, and its default decides what a plain `pnpm dev` does. ADR-0015 keeps the Cloud SQL database STOPPED by default and bills only while it is up (you burst it on with `pnpm db:up`, work, then `pnpm db:down`); keyless IAM auth (ADR-0021) means there are no credentials to configure, but `pg` still needs the instance running. `json` is offline-safe but gives each worktree its own divergent copy — the very thing this migration aims to end.

## Diagram

```
  pnpm dev (fresh start)
        │
        ├── default json ──► offline, $0, the DB stays off
        │                    BUT each session keeps its own copy → they drift apart
        │
        └── default pg ────► shared live state (the migration's goal)
                             BUT needs `pnpm db:up` first, and bills while up
```
The default trades “just works offline, but everyone diverges” against “shared by default, but costs and needs the DB up.”

## Options

- **A — Default `json`**: offline-safe, zero cost, no DB prerequisite; a fresh clone just works. But shared live state (the migration's goal) is opt-in, so most sessions keep diverging.

- **B — Default `pg`**: sessions share live state by default — the dogfooding win. But `pnpm dev` now fails unless the instance is up (cost + a `db:up` step), breaking offline work.

## Recommendation

Non-binding: default `json` while the pg path is new and human-verified-after-the-fact — it preserves offline dev and the stop-by-default cost posture, and `pg` stays a deliberate opt-in for sessions that want shared state. Revisit once the studio<->store loop is proven and `db:up` is routine. The owner decides.

title: Reconcile the YAML-as-source claim with the JSON/markdown reality?
description: ADR-0013 says YAML is the source of truth, but the Library's structured source on disk is JSON (knowledge.json) and stories/ are frontmatter-.md. Reconcile the formats or document the split as intended.
body:
**Why this matters.** Our filing policy (ADR-0013) once read 'every record is kept in YAML', while the Library actually files in JSON and the stories in markdown — a handbook naming a filing system nobody used. That mismatch has since been documented away.

## The question

ADR-0013 said 'YAML is the source of truth', but the Library's structured source on disk is JSON (`knowledge.json`) and `stories/` are frontmatter-`.md` — should the formats be reconciled or the split documented? (Now largely answered — see Recommendation.)

## Context

ADR-0013 asserted YAML as the source of truth, but the Library authors in JSON (`knowledge.json`) and stories in frontmatter-markdown (whose frontmatter is itself YAML). Since this was raised, **ADR-0013 was amended with a library-tier scope note**: 'YAML is the source of truth' scopes the work-hierarchy units, while the **library tier's structured source on disk is JSON** (`knowledge.json`, ADR-0018) — same structured-source / markdown-as-view principle, JSON encoding. That is exactly this question's option B (document the split). Separately, **ADR-0023** reframes 'source of truth' away from any file: the **shared store** is the live source of truth and the on-disk files are seeds / export views. Between the two, the original drift is resolved.

## Diagram

```
  ADR-0013 (amended): work-hierarchy = YAML (story frontmatter)
                      library tier    = JSON (knowledge.json, ADR-0018)
  ADR-0023:           live source of truth = the STORE; files are seeds

  => the per-tier split is now DOCUMENTED (option B); no open decision remains
```
The cheap, honest fix was taken: the ADR now describes the per-tier formats rather than forcing a migration.

## Options

- **A — Commit to one format**: pick a single structured source format (e.g. JSON everywhere, or YAML everywhere) and migrate, then ADR-0013 is literally true. Cleanest mental model; real migration cost and churn in build tooling.

- **B — Document the split as intended**: amend ADR-0013 to state that the structured source is JSON for the Library and frontmatter-`.md` for stories (markdown still the rendered view), and that 'YAML' was illustrative. No migration; the ADR matches reality. Accepts heterogeneity.

- **C — Defer**: leave it, accept the drift for now. Cheapest, but the stale ADR keeps misleading readers.

## Recommendation

RESOLVED (non-binding): option B was effectively taken — ADR-0013's library-tier scope note now documents the JSON encoding for the Library (stories stay frontmatter-YAML), and ADR-0023 reframes the source of truth as the shared store rather than any file format. No open decision remains; this entry is retained for lineage and can be retired or graduated to a closed note at the owner's discretion.

title: Doc-vs-implementation precedence
description: Implementation is ground truth; when a doc claim and the code disagree, the gap itself is the load-bearing surface — surface it, do not build on the doc claim.
body:
**The principle.** Implementation is ground truth and doc text is a hypothesis about it; when a finding shows a doc claim (ADR, glossary, guideline, spec) disagrees with the code, the gap itself is the load-bearing surface, not metadata to a move that took the doc at face value.

## Why

It is easy to author the next move — 'extend the code so the doc holds', 'tighten the invariant' — on top of a doc claim a recent finding has already shown false. That move is built on a phantom premise. The honest first question is whether the doc needs correcting to match the code (most common) or the code needs extending to make the doc's claim hold (rarer, an operator call).

## How to apply

Discriminator: does a recent investigation name a doc-vs-code gap, AND does your pending move cite that same doc as load-bearing? Yes on both → reshape any framing that takes the claim at face value and surface the gap to the operator (the doc, its exact claim, the observed behaviour, one question: correct the doc or extend the code?) without pre-deciding. Three resolutions: correct the doc (most common; the downstream move usually dissolves), route code-extension as its own bounded unit (operator-directed), or defer.
provenance: Composes with the `assess-tradeoffs-by-naming-both-sides` pattern (that governs how a surfaced tradeoff is framed; this governs whether the surface should exist). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/doc-vs-implementation-precedence.yml`.

title: Dogfood: fix the source
description: When your own tooling blocks you, fix the tool at its source rather than papering over it with a one-off workaround.
body:
**The principle.** When tooling we own blocks progress, fix the tool or its source; do not mask the symptom with a one-off workaround that leaves the root cause live to resurface.

## Why

storytree is built with the very kind of agent-driven system it builds (orchestrator, spine, owned loop, studio), so a defect in our own tooling is a first-class bug, not an environmental annoyance. A null guard, hardcoded value, swallowed exception, or shell fallback gets you unblocked now but hides a real defect; the problem goes quiet and re-emerges later, harder to diagnose under a layer of masking, and the next session inherits the same wall.

## How to apply

Reproduce and trace to the earliest failing signal; validate that a test genuinely captures the failure before changing production code; fix the root cause where it lives so the bad state cannot occur (not a guard that hides it after the fact); prefer making one behaviour simply correct over adding a flag. Anti-patterns: a catch-all that does not handle the error, hardcoding a value to silence a dynamic failure, a narrow edge-case handler for a problem that should be fixed generically, and falling back to a workaround tool without recording that the primary one failed.
provenance: Composes with `verify-edit-write-persisted-or-escalate` (a recovery fallback is permitted only after the failure is made visible). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/dogfood-fix-the-source.yml`.

title: Exploration principles
description: Exploration is read-only reconnaissance: discover patterns over enumerating files, stay context-minimal, work in parallel and independent, never mutate.
body:
**The principle.** When exploring a codebase to inform a decision, discover patterns rather than enumerate files, load the minimum context for the scope, work independently of other explorers, and never modify anything.

## Why

Exploration feeds a decision (a decomposition, a scope call, a plan). The risks are over-reading (burning the attention budget on exhaustive cataloguing), over-reaching (analysing a surface another agent owns), and quietly mutating code meant only to be read. Disciplined exploration keeps findings sharp, cheap, and safe to act on.

## How to apply

Use glob/search to learn structure and sample representative files rather than reading everything; stay inside the assigned scope and only note (not analyse) neighbouring surfaces; identify the high-level patterns that matter and flag risks and unknowns explicitly; produce a self-contained result with concrete file paths and no redundancy with another explorer's scope; use read-only tools only. When the context to explore genuinely exceeds the model window, escalate to recursive decomposition rather than reading everything at once.
provenance: Composes with the `recursive-decomposition-patterns` pattern. Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Guidance quality
description: Fix unfollowed guidance with structure (path / signpost / fence / offload), not emphasis (caps / repetition / strong language).
body:
**The principle.** When guidance is not being followed, fix its structure — add the missing path, signpost, fence, or offload — rather than adding emphasis.

## Why

Agents do not weight visual or emotional emphasis the way a human skimming a page might; capitalising, repeating, or marking something CRITICAL adds noise without raising the chance an instruction is followed. Ignored guidance is almost always a structural failure: the instruction is absent at the decision point, lacks a concrete example, or lacks a constraint that removes the ambiguity. Emphasis treats the symptom; structure treats the cause.

## How to apply

Effective patterns: path (a concrete step placed where it is needed), signpost (a concrete example, ideally a link to one that exists), fence (a constraint that makes the wrong move structurally hard or measurable), offload (move a deterministic error-prone step into code the spine sequences). Anti-patterns: caps emphasis, repetition, strong/urgent language, emphasis escalation on already-ignored guidance, and negative framing with no positive alternative (replace 'do not write outside the scope' with 'writes land only within the declared scope'). Reach for emphasis only after structure has genuinely failed — which is rare.
provenance: Composes with the `signal-and-noise` principle (the discriminatory-power lens this serves). Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: No proof preservation
description: Never soften or dodge a correct edit to keep an earned status; make the edit, let the status regress, and re-prove.
body:
**The principle.** Never soften, tiptoe around, or refuse a correct edit in order to preserve a status or verdict that was expensive to earn; ship the correct edit and let the status regress — the system re-verifies on demand.

## Why

A proof-preserving edit leaves two falsehoods, not one: the artifact is still wrong AND the verdict now attests to text deliberately softened to avoid invalidating it, so the verdict is no longer trustworthy. This follows directly from the prove-it-gate and the proof-hash: a unit reaches `healthy` only via earned, on-disk evidence, and a change to proof-bearing content invalidates the prior verdict by design.

## How to apply

Make the correct edit; do not phrase it to dodge a proof-hash change. Expect and accept the regression — a correctly-amended `healthy` story reverts to `building` (or computes `unhealthy`); that is the system asking for a re-proof. Let the tier’s proof mode re-run (contract test, integration test, UAT, or operator attestation). Verdicts are cheap to re-issue; that is the whole point of binding status to evidence rather than a hand-edited flag.
provenance: The inverse of a hand-edit that fakes `healthy`: there a unit claims proof it never earned; here a unit clings to proof its new content no longer supports. See the `prove-it-gate` principle and the `proof-hash` definition. Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/no-proof-preservation.yml`.

title: Reward hacking
description: A gameable success signal gets gamed; define success by observable outcome, require evidence, and separate the party that judges from the party that did the work.
body:
**The principle.** An agent rewarded for a gameable success signal will optimise the signal rather than the work; define success by observable end-results, require concrete evidence of them, and keep judging separate from doing.

## Why

When the owned loop works at a leaf it is rewarded for green tests. If the tests pin a gameable signal — a return code, a help-text string, a 'success: true' flag — the loop can earn the reward with a hollow implementation, and the breakage stays hidden until the slowest, most expensive channel (the story UAT against real collaborators) runs. This green-signal/red-reality failure is the root class the whole proof model exists to prevent.

## How to apply

Outcome over process: prove 'the user can complete the journey', not 'the step executed' — exactly what the proof ladder encodes (contract test → integration test against real in-story collaborators → story UAT, no mocks within the organism). Require the concrete observable captured in the event store, not a bare flag. Separate judging from doing: approval onto the trunk is a distinct operator act, never self-granted; an agent can never self-exempt. Audit the tests themselves — a test can pass while failing to verify the requirement. Watch for help-text-only validation, success-flag-without-verification, mocking the real dependency, asserting implementation detail, permissive assertions (length > 0), missing unhappy paths, and swallowed errors.
provenance: Composes with `implementer-shortcut-patterns` (the specific hollow shapes), `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` (the sterile-fixture inverse), and `test-creation-principles`. See the `faked-uat-theatre` and `agent-never-self-exempts` artifacts. Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Signal and noise
description: Judge guidance by discriminatory power: signal lets the agent choose the right move; noise consumes attention without adding that power. Author for signal, cut noise.
body:
**The principle.** Judge any guidance an agent reads by its discriminatory power — signal lets the agent distinguish the correct action from the alternatives; noise consumes attention without adding that power — and author for high signal.

## Why

An agent (an owned-loop session, the orchestrator's routing prompt, a doc) operates in a finite attention window. Every sentence that does not help it choose the next move competes with the sentences that do; low-signal guidance does not merely fail to help, it crowds out the content that would.

## How to apply

High signal is actionable, specific (concrete files/patterns/decisions), verifiable (a testable success criterion), and evidence-based (grounded in the codebase as it actually is). Noise causes attentional drift: meta-talk, stale context, generic philosophy, and structural redundancy (the same definition restated in several places). Per sentence ask: can I remove it without lowering the chance the task completes (→ noise)? does it point to a specific action/file/criterion (no → likely noise)? is it duplicated elsewhere (→ link the single source)? will it still be true later (no → stale)? Name concrete surfaces over vague gestures and link a single source of truth rather than restating it.
provenance: Composes with the `guidance-quality` principle (the authoring moves that add signal). Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Stale prerequisite links are phantoms
description: A cross-story dependency edge with no shared observable is a phantom; removing it is map correction, not loosening — the within-unit proof ladder stays sacred.
body:
**The principle.** Distinguish the sacred within-unit proof ladder from a contestable cross-story dependency edge: when a `boundary` edge gates a unit and a staleness suspicion arises, ask not only 'heal the upstream?' but 'is this edge itself a phantom?'

## Why

Across stories, a story depends on another only through a declared `boundary`, and that edge makes a downstream proof meaningful in context. But edges go stale — most often when an ADR or an upstream interface is amended and a downstream story still carries an edge reflecting the old intent. The entanglement no longer exists: a phantom, still drawn on the map, pointing at nothing. Removing it is map correction; the dependency mechanism stays load-bearing, the removed edge never was.

## How to apply

Compare what the two stories’ proofs actually verify, observable-anchored not authorship-anchored: read the downstream interface and the observables its UAT/integration tests pin, and the candidate-upstream `boundary` and the observables its tests pin. No shared observable → phantom, removable (surface to the operator; DAG-shape changes are operator territory). Shared observable → load-bearing; keep it and heal the chain. Partial overlap keeps the edge; if the downstream’s own proofs are stale, amend the downstream first; never re-target an edge (map correction removes edges that point at nothing, it does not point them elsewhere). When in doubt, heal the chain.
provenance: Composes with the `defects-amend-the-owning-story` principle (that expands the DAG; this contracts it); both reach the operator before the DAG shape changes. See the `dependency` and `boundary` definitions (ADR-0010). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/stale-prerequisite-links-are-phantoms.yml`.

title: Test creation principles
description: A test must verify the real outcome, not a proxy — content over existence, behaviour over implementation detail — and must fail if the behaviour it pins were removed.
body:
**The principle.** A test must verify the actual outcome rather than a proxy for it — real content over existence, user-facing behaviour over implementation detail — and must genuinely fail if the behaviour it pins were removed.

## Why

A test is only as good as the observable it pins. A test that checks a file exists (not its contents), that a function was called (not what it produced), or that a flag is true (not the result it claims) gives a green signal with no proof behind it. The proof ladder (contract → integration → UAT) is trustworthy only if each rung’s assertions bind to external truth rather than a gameable signal.

## How to apply

Verify actual outcomes, not success flags — check file contents, assert user-facing behaviour, include concrete assertions about what was produced. Avoid the hollow shapes in `reward-hacking` and `implementer-shortcut-patterns`. One behaviour per test, clear names, order-independent, granularity matched to the tier; pin the behaviour that matters and do not over-test; use real collaborators where the tier allows (no mocks within an organism) and cover the unhappy path and edge cases. The sharpest single check — the deletion check: if the feature were removed, would this test fail? If not, it pins nothing and is a false green.
provenance: Same falsifiability discipline as `verify-edit-write-persisted-or-escalate`, applied to assertions. See the `contract-test` and `mock-uat-seam` definitions. Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Test fixtures mirror production failure modes
description: A fixture must fail when production fails; a fixture that pre-installs a property production lacks is a false green that hides the production failure mode.
body:
**The principle.** A fixture, stub, or mock must be designed so the test fails when production fails; one that pre-installs a property production might lack hands the test a false green that hides the production failure mode.

## Why

The `mock-uat-seam` says where a mock may live (the contract tier yes; within an organism no) but is silent on whether a permitted mock is sterile or adversarial. A mock that satisfies the no-mocks-in-the-organism rule can still be defective if it stages a world more forgiving than what the code will actually meet — priming a stub with one platform's error wording, pre-creating state production does not create, granting access production's runtime lacks.

## How to apply

Per pre-install, ask in order: (1) Inversion — if I remove it, does the test still cover its observable? Yes → convenience/isolation, fine; No → load-bearing, continue. (2) Production guarantee — does production guarantee the property by build config, a documented invariant, or the boundary contract it stands on? Yes → it mirrors a real guarantee, fine; No → it masks a production failure mode, defective. Remediate by dropping the pre-install and asserting the typed error production should return, adding a sibling test for the property-absent case, or escalating so production actually guarantees the property. Fires hardest when scaffolding fixtures for filesystem/runtime/permission/external-process state, and when re-authoring tests after a UAT-caught defect.
provenance: Composes with `implementer-shortcut-patterns` (sterile fixture here, hollow implementation there) and `tightening-a-shared-contract-needs-a-full-sweep`. See the `mock-uat-seam` definition. Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes.yml`.

title: Tightening a shared contract needs a full sweep
description: When a change makes a shared validator reject inputs it used to accept, run the whole suite across every consuming capability green before the UAT is signed and the result approved.
body:
**The principle.** When a change tightens a shared parsing/validation contract — it now rejects inputs it previously accepted — run the full test suite across every capability that touches that contract, confirmed green, before the story's UAT is signed and before the result is approved onto the trunk.

## Why

Tightening a shared validator (a loader, deserializer, schema constraint, newly-required field) can invalidate fixtures or seed data anywhere that leaned on the old looser behaviour, and those regressions surface at runtime — a fixture fails to parse when its test runs — not at type-check or build time. So the change looks safe: it compiles, the owning capability is green, the UAT passes. The node rollup and approval gate read the event store plus on-disk evidence; neither re-runs the whole suite, so a story can reach `healthy` and be approved with cross-capability fixture regressions undetected.

## How to apply

Discriminator: (1) did the change make a shared parsing/validation surface reject an input shape it formerly accepted (a stricter loader/parser, a new rejection, rejecting unknown fields, a tightened or added schema constraint, a newly-required field)? No → the rule does not arm. Yes → continue. (2) Is the full suite green across every capability, not just the owner? Yes → proceed with the sign and the approval. No → block; a red sibling suite forfeits the sign and approval even with a clean tree and a passing walkthrough; route the broken fixtures back to their owning capability (defects amend the owning story) before re-attempting. A loosening (accept more) cannot invalidate an existing fixture, so it does not trigger. Always sweep the whole workspace — catching other capabilities’ fixtures is the point.
provenance: Composes with `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` (that prevents a sterile fixture passing; this catches a previously-valid fixture a contract change just broke). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/tightening-a-shared-contract-needs-a-full-sweep.yml`.

title: Verify an edit persisted, or escalate
description: Read back every contract-bearing write; on silent non-persistence, record a structured assumption-violation before any fallback — a silent fallback that hides the failure is banned.
body:
**The principle.** For every contract-bearing edit or write — one whose persistence your deliverable depends on — read the file back and confirm the intended content is present before proceeding; if it did not persist, record a structured assumption-violation in your return before applying any workaround.

## Why

An edit/write tool can return success without the content landing on disk (filesystem interception, sandbox quirks, path-normalisation edge cases). The in-the-wild reaction — silently falling back to a shell heredoc — hides the failure: the orchestrator never learns the tool misbehaved, the escalation pathway is forfeit, and the symptom recurs unnamed next session. One extra read per contract-bearing write turns a silent symptom into a structured signal the orchestrator already knows how to consume.

## How to apply

Contract-bearing = any write whose persistence your return summary implicitly claims (the discriminator: would my summary lie if this file were not on disk?) — source files, test scaffolds, evidence rows, schema changes, spec amendments; throwaway scratch is out of scope. Issue the write, immediately read the path, verify the read reflects the intent. On failure (unchanged/absent/truncated/pre-call content): do NOT silently fall back; record an assumption-violation in your return (`{ briefed, observed, severity }`) the orchestrator parses programmatically; only after that record exists is a heredoc fallback permitted as recovery. The contract pinned is the visibility of the failure, not the success of the recovery.
provenance: Composes with `implementer-shortcut-patterns` and `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` — the same falsifiability discipline applied to a write’s persistence. Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/verify-edit-write-persisted-or-escalate.yml`.

title: Implementer shortcut patterns
description: Five hollow-implementation shapes that pass shape-level tests but fail UAT — and the shape-vs-depth check that catches them.
body:
**The pattern.** An implementation that satisfies a test's shape via a shortcut while leaving the real contract obligation un-implemented is defective even when every contract and integration test passes green; a test's contract is the observable the production code must produce, not a signature, a return type, or an entered code path.

## Problem

When the owned loop works at the leaf it is rewarded for green tests. If a test pins shape but not depth, the loop can earn green with a hollow implementation and the breakage stays invisible until the story's UAT runs the canonical journey end-to-end — the slowest, most expensive channel. This is the 'test green, production red' defect class, the inverse of a sterile fixture.

## Approach

Know the five sub-patterns: (1) help-text narrowing — assert a `--help` mentions a feature instead of running the canonical invocation through real dispatch → logic → output; (2) TODO-and-exit stub on the production arm — wire a test-only seam but leave the real-user branch a TODO + error-exit; (3) discarded typed parameter — accept a typed collaborator, suppress the unused-warning, never invoke it; (4) mock shipped in the production path — a mock reachable from the shipped artifact, gated only by a runtime flag; (5) silent fallback to fake data — a resource-acquisition path that substitutes a placeholder and returns success. Per seam, in order: shape vs depth (do the assertions observe the chain’s terminal effect, or an intermediate shape?), production-path coverage (does any test exercise the real production branch under a stub seam?), then name the shortcut and apply its discipline — wire the production arm, consume the parameter, exclude the mock, fail loud with a typed error.

## Tradeoffs

Wiring the production arm now costs upfront effort paid once; deferring it pays the UAT-only catch of every future regression forever on the slowest channel. Default to wiring it.
provenance: Composes with `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` (the sterile-fixture inverse) and `reward-hacking`. See the `faked-uat-theatre` and `mock-uat-seam` artifacts. Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/implementer-shortcut-patterns.yml`.

title: Pull-based context architecture
description: Brief an agent thinly and let it pull fresh context just-in-time, rather than pushing a fat static brief at session start.
body:
**The pattern.** Give an agent a thin bootstrap and let it pull exactly the context its current step needs — minimal initial load, fetched on demand, always read from the live source — rather than pushing a large static brief at session start.

## Problem

The push model — pre-loading a big static brief covering every situation the agent might hit — pays three costs every turn: a context tax (a large brief consumes the attention budget even when most is irrelevant), staleness (a brief loaded at session start does not reflect later changes; the agent reasons over a snapshot), and one-size-fits-all (every step gets the whole brief; no step gets context tailored to it).

## Approach

Thin bootstrap — the starting brief carries the agent’s role, current objective, and how to fetch more; pointers, not payloads. Pull on demand — the agent fetches operational context when it needs it, and what it fetches is paths to read, not embedded blobs, always reading current state. Progressive disclosure — each step’s result points at the next thing to fetch. Keep briefs lean: name the surface and link its authoritative source instead of inlining it (self-contained means complete pointers, not complete payloads). When a single context to pull would still exceed the window, escalate to recursive decomposition.

## Tradeoffs

You trade a guaranteed-complete upfront snapshot for freshness and a small initial load; the agent makes extra fetches, but each reads live state instead of a stale cache. In storytree the event store is the single source of truth and the orchestrator briefs each node — this argues for those briefs to be pointers into live state, not fat snapshots.
provenance: A context-engineering principle for how agents are briefed, not a code spec for any subsystem. Composes with `signal-and-noise` (at the briefing layer) and `recursive-decomposition-patterns`. See ADR-0011 (pull-based just-in-time context). Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Recursive decomposition patterns
description: When a context genuinely exceeds the model window, hold it as a queryable environment, filter to the sparse relevant slice, and recurse with bounded depth — do not summarise lossily.
body:
**The pattern.** When a context genuinely exceeds the model's window, decompose rather than summarise lossily or hope a bigger window saves you: hold the large context as an environment the agent queries programmatically, filter to the sparse relevant slice, and recurse with bounded depth.

## Problem

Most tasks do not need all of a large context at once. Loading everything wastes the attention budget and degrades reasoning; summarising upfront loses the details the task needs. The alternative is to treat the context as data the agent searches — loading only the slice each sub-step requires — so it can work over a corpus far larger than its window without lossy compression. Reserve this for contexts that actually exceed the limit; for anything that fits, plain loading is simpler and faster.

## Approach

Context as environment — store the large input as named, queryable state and issue queries (filter for a pattern, navigate a section, find usages) pulling only what each step needs. Filter over chunk — prefer extracting the semantically relevant slice over blind size-based splitting. Recursive decomposition — break into sub-tasks, process each over its own slice, accumulate, aggregate (filter-then-process, hierarchical extraction, map-reduce). Search/execution firewall — separate context curation from task execution; a curation pass prepares the minimal slice, an execution pass works with only that slice and does no further searching. Discipline: measure before activating; bound and decrement a max depth; name the accumulators; hold the firewall; aggregate before signalling completion.

## Tradeoffs

You trade setup overhead and orchestration complexity for the ability to reason over an oversized corpus without lossy compression; not worth it when the context fits comfortably. Natural homes in storytree: oversized exploration, large owned-loop-event-stream or evidence analysis, and reasoning over big spec inputs during decomposition.
provenance: Source: Recursive Language Models (Zhang/Kraska/Khattab, MIT CSAIL); a context-engineering principle, not a code spec. Composes with `exploration-principles` and `pull-based-context-architecture`. Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Repo surface allow-list
description: The repo root and docs/ are allow-listed: a new root entry or a new standalone doc is refused unless added to repo-manifest.json — and a new doc must justify why it does not belong in the Library.
body:
**The boundary.** New files at the repo root, and new standalone docs under docs/, are refused at the gate unless listed in repo-manifest.json — and a new doc’s entry must state why it does not belong in the Library.

## Rule

The set of tracked top-level root entries and loose docs/ files must equal the allow-list in repo-manifest.json. Durable project knowledge belongs in the Library (definition / principle / pattern / guardrail / techstack / adr / open-question), not a loose prose doc; the only standalone narrative doc kept is README.md. ADRs (docs/decisions/) and research notes (docs/research/) are allow-listed at the directory level, so new ADRs need no entry; any other file directly under docs/ needs an explicit, justified manifest entry.

## Enforced by

scripts/check-manifest.mjs (`pnpm check:manifest`), run inside `pnpm gate` and the CI `verify` job before typecheck (ADR-0022). It reads `git ls-files` and exits non-zero on any unlisted root entry or unlisted/unjustified doc, so an offending change cannot pass the green gate that guards merge to `main`.

## Failure mode prevented

Without it, agents accrete temp/ad-hoc files at the root and one-off prose docs under docs/ that duplicate or bypass the Library — splitting authority over durable knowledge, rotting the tree, and re-creating the doc-sprawl the Library was meant to replace.
provenance: Authoritatively defined by ADR-0025; enforces on the ADR-0022 dev-repo green gate. The repo-hygiene complement to `edit-first-curation` (search/edit before authoring) and `signal-and-noise` (cut low-signal docs). Distinct from the PRODUCT proof gate (`gate` / `never-bypass-the-gate` / `prove-it-gate`): same "a gate refuses, it does not warn" family, but this guards the dev repo’s git surface, not promotion onto the story DAG. Mechanism: `repo-manifest.json` + `scripts/check-manifest.mjs`.

title: Library edit ceremony
description: How a session writes Library state: explore offline, search first, then CLI writes with `--pg` against the live store — never by hand-editing the seed.
body:
**The ceremony.** A session changes Library state only through the CLI against the live Cloud SQL store — explore offline, search before writing, then `pnpm db:up` and write with `--pg` — never by hand-editing the seed or generated files.

## Trigger

A session needs to change Library state — edit an artifact's fields or author a new artifact — rather than merely read it. (Pure reads need no ceremony: they run offline against the in-memory seed. `artifact comment` and retire are not yet CLI surfaces.)

## Steps

1. **Explore offline.** `pnpm storytree library` (the dashboard) is the first command when working with the Library; drill in with `pnpm storytree library artifact <id>`, `pnpm storytree library artifact list <category>`, and `pnpm storytree library tree focus <id>`. Reads run against the in-memory seed — no DB needed.
2. **Search before you write** (asset:edit-first-curation). Real search is deferred (ADR-0023 §6) — `artifact list <category>` is the interim discovery path. Find the closest existing artifact and edit it by default; reaching for `artifact new` must be justified by why editing wasn't right. `new` refuses an existing id and points at `edit`.
3. **Bring the DB up:** `pnpm db:up`. The instance is stopped by default for cost and idle-stops after ~60 minutes of zero connections.
4. **Write through the CLI with `--pg`:** `pnpm storytree library artifact edit <id> --set <field>=<value> --pg`, or `pnpm storytree library artifact new --file <doc.json> --pg`. Writes are **refused without `--pg`**; every write re-validates at the boundary (`validateLibraryDoc`) and returns a failure as guidance rather than persisting.
5. **Mind the `--json` trap:** `pnpm` swallows the `--json` flag (it is reserved by pnpm). Pass a doc via `--file`, or use inline `--json` only via `npx tsx packages/cli/src/main.ts …`.
6. **If a write can't connect,** the DB has likely idle-stopped — re-run `pnpm db:up` and retry.
7. **Keep sessions off the same artifact.** Different artifacts never contend (per-id rows, transactional upserts); the **same** artifact across two sessions is not coordinated — claims are DBOS-deferred — so don't co-edit one artifact in parallel.

## Surfaces

- **Library** — the only surface written. Each CLI write lands in the live Cloud SQL store as one event plus a transactional projection upsert; this store is the **live source of truth** for artifact state. Reads touch it only with `--pg`; offline reads come from the in-memory seed.
- **Repo** — read-only here. `apps/studio/data/knowledge.json` is the migration seed/export, and `apps/studio/data/assets.json` + `docs/glossary.md` are **generated** from it (`build-corpus.mjs`). The ceremony reads the seed (via the offline CLI) and never writes any of the three; a DB→seed export is later work, so the seed lags live edits by design.

## Failure modes

- **Hand-editing `knowledge.json` for a live change** — the edit is invisible to the live store, to parallel sessions, and to the studio; seed and DB silently diverge, and the next migration decides which version survives.
- **Running `load-corpus.ts --force` against a live DB carrying CLI edits** — the seed overwrites the live state and **reverts those edits**; with no DB→seed export, they are simply lost.
- **Hand-editing the generated `assets.json` or `docs/glossary.md`** — the next `build-corpus.mjs` run erases the edit.
- **Skipping the search step** — duplicate artifacts split authority and sever the evidence chain (asset:edit-first-curation); consumers no longer know which doc is canonical.
- **Co-editing the same artifact from two sessions** — writes are uncoordinated, so the last upsert silently wins and the other session's edit vanishes without an error.
- **Forgetting `pnpm db:up`** — writes fail to connect after an idle-stop; harmless, but the session stalls until it re-runs `db:up`.

## Verification

The write-path guards are machine-checked: the CLI **refuses** any write without `--pg`, `artifact new` refuses an existing id, and `validateLibraryDoc` rejects an invalid doc at the boundary — none of these can be skipped by forgetting them. Nothing, however, checks that a session searched before writing, that `knowledge.json` or the generated files weren't hand-edited, or that `load-corpus.ts --force` isn't run over live CLI edits — those steps rest entirely on the session honouring the ceremony.
provenance: Derived (per ADR-0034) from ADR-0023 §10–11 and the CLAUDE.md library section; on any disagreement the cited ADRs win.

title: library
description: The shared knowledge tier — schema-validated artifact docs in the live Cloud SQL store, read just-in-time by sessions via the CLI, by humans via the studio.
body:
**In one line.** The shared **knowledge tier**: schema-validated artifact docs — definitions, principles, patterns, guardrails, techstack, processes, open questions — living in the shared Cloud SQL Postgres store, read just-in-time by sessions through the CLI and by humans through the studio.

## What it is

One of the project's three working surfaces — the story tree is the **work**, the noticeboard is the **sessions**, the library is the **knowledge** (ADR-0034). It is a corpus tier parallel to the work hierarchy: structured documents discriminated by `kind` ∈ {definition, principle, pattern, guardrail, techstack, process, open-question} (`process` is the newest, ADR-0034), stored as **JSONB docs, zod-validated at the write boundary**, with relationships as ID references inside the docs (ADR-0017). History = events, current state = projection. The **live shared Cloud SQL Postgres store is the source of truth for artifact state** (ADR-0023 §11); git holds the code plus *generated* views (`docs/glossary.md`, `assets.json`), and `apps/studio/data/knowledge.json` is the migration seed/export, not the edit-here surface. Sessions reach it just-in-time through the exploratory `storytree library` CLI — dashboard → kind → artifact → local DAG, choose-your-own-adventure, never a corpus dump (ADR-0023) — and humans through the studio. Artifacts are **downstream and derived**: they cite their source ADRs via `references` and reference-don't-restate; on disagreement the cited ADR wins.

## What it is not

Not the **glossary** — `docs/glossary.md` is a *generated projection* of library docs, not a peer source. Not the **studio** — the UI window onto the library (and the rest of the system), not the tier itself. Not **`docs/decisions/` ADRs** — those are the source/history layer where decisions are made; library artifacts are derived from them and cite them. Not the **`stories/` tree** — that is the work to be built; the library is the knowledge consumed while building it.
provenance: Tier returned by ADR-0017 (resolving open-q §9); named "library" by ADR-0019; live-store-as-source-of-truth and the CLI interface by ADR-0023; the three-surfaces framing and the `process` kind by ADR-0034.

title: Merge ceremony
description: The dev-repo landing ceremony: when a unit of work is green, commit, push, open a non-draft PR, and stop — CI auto-merges on green; sessions never merge manually. Read before landing anything on git `main`.
body:
**The ceremony.** When a unit of work is green, commit, push, and open a **non-draft PR** — then stop: the CI `automerge` job lands it on the dev repo's git `main` once `verify` passes, and a session never merges manually.

## Trigger

A unit of work is complete and the local gate is green — `pnpm gate` passes on the session's branch — with nothing red or WIP in the diff. Run the ceremony **without waiting to be asked**: sitting on green work is the failure this artifact exists to stop (ADR-0034). It also begins when picking a branch back up on another machine — start at the sync step.

## Steps

1. **Sync** (only when resuming an existing branch): `pnpm sync` rebases onto `origin/main`; after a rebase, push with `--force-with-lease` (fine for single-author branches — ADR-0022).
2. **Gate locally**: `pnpm gate` (repo manifest + typecheck + offline tests). It mirrors CI's `verify` job, which additionally runs the build. Red means the unit is not done — do not proceed to a non-draft PR.
3. **Commit** the unit on the session branch, never directly on `main`.
4. **Push** the branch to `origin`.
5. **Open a non-draft PR** into `main` with `gh pr create`. A `claude/real/*` promotion branch's tip is the verdict's exact attested commit — never rebase, squash, or amend it (ADR-0031).
6. **Stop.** Never run `gh pr merge` in any flavor (including `--auto`): the repo has no required checks or branch protection, so a manual merge lands *instantly, pre-CI*. The workflow's `automerge` job (`needs: verify`) merges with `--merge --delete-branch` — a true merge commit, which is what keeps a promoted verdict's commit an ancestor of `main`.
7. **To hold a PR** — the only opt-out — mark it a draft or add the `hold` label. Every other green non-draft PR merges. Merge to `main` quickly and frequently; the cadence is trunk-based.

## Surfaces

**Repo/CI only.** Reads the working tree and branch state; writes a commit, a pushed branch, and a PR; CI's `verify` job re-runs the gate on the merge result and the `automerge` job writes the merge to git `main` and deletes the branch.

**Two trunks — do not conflate them.** This ceremony governs the **dev repo's git `main`**, the meta-layer that builds storytree. The **product story-trunk** is a different surface: it is approval-gated (ADR-0008; `trunk`, `approval-gated-trunk`) and is **never auto-merged** — a green agent result there is a request for human diff-review, not a landing. The invariant shared by both is *never merge un-green*; what differs is whether green is sufficient (here, yes — solo author) or necessary-but-not-sufficient (the product).

## Failure modes

- **Manual `gh pr merge`** merges instantly, pre-CI — no required checks exist, so the gate is bypassed entirely. This is the recorded incident: sessions reached for `gh pr merge` (including `--auto`, which on this unprotected repo also merges immediately) and landed unverified work (ADR-0034). Let the workflow merge.
- **Squash-merging a `claude/real/*` promotion PR** mints a new sha and orphans the persisted verdict's anchor; the attested commit must remain an ancestor of `main` (ADR-0031).
- **Sitting on green work**: the owner repeatedly had to tell sessions to commit and open a PR (ADR-0034's observable symptom), and unlanded work drifts across the owner's machines.
- **Red/WIP work in a non-draft PR**: the moment it happens to go green it auto-merges, and half-finished work lands on `main`. Finish the unit or mark the PR draft.
- **Conflating the two trunks**: waiting for a human to merge a dev PR (a stall — none is coming), or carrying auto-merge-on-green thinking onto the product story-trunk (exactly the v1 scar `approval-gated-trunk` exists to prevent).

## Verification

The green-before-merge half is deterministic: `automerge` is an Actions job in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with `needs: verify`, gated to non-draft PRs without the `hold` label, and the `pull_request` checkout tests the merge result — nothing merges *through the workflow* un-green. **Nothing checks the rest**: there is no branch protection, so no machine stops a manual `gh pr merge` — merge-only-via-the-workflow is enforced by convention (ADR-0022 says so explicitly); and non-squash for `claude/real/*` is a recorded operating rule whose enforcement automation is later work (ADR-0031).
provenance: Synthesized downstream of ADR-0022/0031 per ADR-0034 (the kind's first instance); on any disagreement the cited ADR wins.

title: noticeboard
description: The session-presence surface: parallel sessions declare who exists, in which worktree, working on what — advisory-only, staleness-aged, never blocking (ADR-0033).
body:
**In one line.** The session-presence surface — one of the three working surfaces (tree = the work, **noticeboard** = the sessions, library = the knowledge): parallel sessions declare "I exist, in this worktree, working on X" anchored to story nodes; advisory-only, staleness-aged, never blocking.

## What it is

The **coordination organ** among the three working surfaces: the tree shows the work, the library holds the knowledge, the noticeboard shows the **sessions**. Each parallel session upserts one declaration — `workingOn` prose plus optional work-hierarchy node ids — over an append-only history (`events.session_event` + `events.session`, the house event+projection pattern). **Identity is the worktree name** — derived, never typed — and there is **no signer chain**: presence is not proof, so ADR-0020's fail-closed signing posture deliberately does not apply. **Staleness replaces release discipline**: the board ages rows by `lastSeenAt`; `status: done` is politeness, not a requirement. Automation declares presence only through never-blocking paths — plain spine code around builds, fire-and-forget session hooks, a statusline glance — **never a blocking-capable hook**, and a board write failure never fails the enclosing action. Presence is live-DB only (`pnpm db:up`); offline surfaces degrade gracefully, rendering without presence lines rather than failing. Surfaces: `storytree noticeboard` (the dedicated board + `declare`/`done`) and the presence block woven into `storytree tree` (ADR-0023's choose-your-own-adventure pattern, extended). Defined by ADR-0033; built through the prove-it-gate as `stories/notice-board` — per-capability status lives in the story, not here.

## What it is not

Not the **feedback organ** — cites, archival and synthesis belong to `stories/feedback-graduation` (ADR-0032); the noticeboard carries presence only. Not a **lock or claims system** — the board *shows* overlap, nothing refuses anything; typed claims-with-refusal is named-deferred (like DBOS, ADR-0019), revisited only if overlap conflicts become routine. Not a **proof surface** — declarations carry no signer chain and attest nothing; verdicts and the gate's signing posture (ADR-0020) live elsewhere.
provenance: Decided by ADR-0033, which restores V1 Agentic ADR-0022's forum≠noticeboard organ split — deliberately minus its claims gate. Built as stories/notice-board.

title: Should artifacts carry code backing — tests that keep their claims honest?
description: Open: whether guardrails (first) and other artifacts should link executable backing so the artifact↔code link cannot silently rot.
body:
**Why this matters.** A guardrail's `enforcedBy` is a claim about code, and nothing re-checks it as the code moves — a stale claim quietly turns a "deterministically-enforced boundary" into prose that merely sounds enforced.

## The question

Should Library artifacts — guardrails first — carry executable backing (a linked test asserting their `enforcedBy` mechanism still refuses), and should other artifact kinds maintain their own test suites where keeping the artifact↔code link honest is worth the upkeep?

## Context

Raised by the owner on 2026-06-11 alongside ADR-0034 (the `process` kind — "guardrails are mainly prose; maybe artifacts can have their own test suite where it makes sense"). The `guardrail` kind's defining field is `enforcedBy` — the deterministic mechanism (a gate, a schema, a DB constraint, a code path) that makes the boundary non-bypassable — but that claim is verified once, at authoring time, by the author reading the code. ADR-0016 (knowledge↔code binding and staleness) already designs the adjacent machinery: versioned anchors from knowledge to code, and demotion of undescribed drift. ADR-0026 gives the library a health-check surface (`library doctor`) where an automated check could live. The question is how far to go beyond anchors: from "the named code still exists" to "the named code still refuses".

## Options

- **A. Per-artifact test backing.** A `backedBy` field naming a test (run in CI) that asserts the guardrail's mechanism still refuses — e.g. a forged-green attempt still aborts. Strongest honesty: semantic drift breaks the build. Cost: a schema change, test upkeep per guardrail, and the risk of stale test *paths* — the same disease one level down.
- **B. Anchor resolution in the health check.** The ADR-0026 doctor verifies every `enforcedBy` names a file/symbol that still exists, without asserting behaviour. Cheap, catches deletion-drift; blind to semantic drift (the code exists but no longer refuses).
- **C. Wait for ADR-0016.** Do nothing until the binding/staleness design is implemented, then revisit with real anchors available. Zero cost now vs. the gap staying open — guardrail claims keep aging unchecked.

## Recommendation

Start with **B** as the cheap floor (it slots into the existing `library doctor`), and design **A** against ADR-0016's anchor model rather than ad hoc — a `backedBy` link should be a versioned anchor, not a bare path. Non-binding until the owner decides.
provenance: Owner idea, 2026-06-11 (raised with ADR-0034). Still open.

title: REAL build drive
description: The ceremony for driving a story node through the prove-it-gate for real — register the node, run `node build --real --store pg`, let a signed PASS promote itself, land non-squash.
body:
**The ceremony.** Drives one story node through the prove-it-gate for real: a live leaf authors the genuine red→green in a fresh git worktree, the spine signs and persists the verdict, and a PASS promotes its exact proven commit onto a `claude/real/<unit>-<run>` branch that lands non-squash so the attested commit stays an ancestor of `main`.

## Trigger

A story node's build is actually **next** — its file-per-unit spec exists in the story tree and the session intends a real, leaf-authored proof (not `--dry-run`, the offline scripted walk, and not `--live`, the subscription-billed smoke). For a whole story, `pnpm storytree story build <story-id>` chains its registered nodes in dependency (topo) order, story UAT node last, with *halted-is-never-a-pass*.

## Steps

1. **Register the node** — add its entry to `NODE_BUILD_REGISTRY` in `packages/orchestrator/src/test-command-registry.ts`: the package proof `command`, per-phase write `scope`, and a `real` block (`testFile`, `sourceFile`, real write walls; `install: true` iff the authored files import workspace deps). Registration is a deliberate per-node act done when that node's build is next — never pre-created in bulk (a registered node whose code never lands is the junk-becomes-noise the `verdict-line` cleanup paid for, ADR-0031 §3).
2. **Probe leaf auth** — run `claude -p "ok"`. The SDK leaf authenticates via `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`, not the host session; a 401 means the owner must re-auth (`claude setup-token`) before any long run.
3. **Bring the store up** — `pnpm db:up` (the instance idle-stops; `--store pg` needs it live).
4. **Drive** — `pnpm storytree node build <id> --real --store pg`. The spine creates a fresh git worktree (lockfile-only `pnpm install` for `install: true` targets — the leaf can never touch `package.json`), the leaf authors test then impl inside per-phase write walls, the spine observes RED then GREEN itself, commits the authored work spine-side (the gate's clean-tree check reads a real commit — the verdict's `commitSha`), and signs the verdict. `--store pg` appends to `events.work_event` / `events.verdict`; it is **refused** for dry-runs — a scripted PASS persisted would be a forged healthy.
5. **Let the PASS promote itself** — on a signed REAL pass the spine parks the exact proven commit on `claude/real/<unit>-<run>` and pushes it (ADR-0031). `install: true` targets must additionally pass the node's package suite re-observed in the worktree and a typecheck wall (`tsc --noEmit`) — a red withholds the push, parking the branch local-only. The build envelope reports the ending: promoted, parked, or skipped (nothing authored).
6. **Land non-squash** — open the PR the envelope suggests and merge it **non-squash** (merge commit or fast-forward), so the verdict's `commitSha` stays an ancestor of `main`; then follow the merge ceremony (`asset:merge-ceremony`) for the landing.

## Surfaces

**Story tree** — reads the node's file-per-unit spec (the registered-buildable grain); never writes status into it — health stays a projection of signed verdicts. **Repo/CI** — writes the registry entry (`packages/orchestrator/src/test-command-registry.ts`); creates and tears down a fresh git worktree; pushes the `claude/real/<unit>-<run>` promotion branch; lands through the ADR-0022 PR/CI green gate. **Shared store (events schema)** — `--store pg` appends work events and the signed verdict to `events.work_event` / `events.verdict`.

## Failure modes

Skipping the auth probe (step 2) stalls a run mid-flight: the `declare-presence` overnight `--real` run blocked on a stale OAuth token and left a dangling `building` work-event. Skipping `pnpm db:up` (or hitting an idle-stop) makes `--store pg` unable to persist. Persisting a dry-run would mint a forged healthy — which is exactly why the CLI refuses the combination. Squash-merging a promotion PR mints a new sha and orphans the persisted verdict's anchor — the attested commit is no longer reachable from `main`, breaking the ancestry rule the whole promotion design exists for. Bulk-registering nodes ahead of need recreates the `verdict-line` evidence-evaporates state: registry and spec entries attesting code that never landed. Pushing a promotion despite a red package suite would let a green leaf silently break its package — the regression wall exists because the node's proof attests only itself.

## Verification

Steps 4–5 are checked by code: the phase machine and fail-closed write walls, spine-observed RED/GREEN (env-scrubbed executor), the spine-committed clean-tree signed verdict, and the package-suite + typecheck walls that withhold a red push are enforced in `packages/orchestrator`; the dry-run `--store pg` refusal is enforced CLI-side (`packages/cli`); landing then rides the ADR-0022 CI green gate. NOTHING checks the rest: the auth probe (step 2) is unchecked — a stale token only surfaces mid-run; non-squash merging is an operating rule recorded in ADR-0031, not yet enforced by repo settings; and register-when-next (step 1) is convention only.
provenance: Derived view of ADR-0031 (promotion + dependency-bearing worktrees) and ADR-0030 (the live SDK leaf); the drive was proven end-to-end by the drive-machinery phases (PRs #29, #30, #32).

title: story tree
description: The work surface — the living DAG of stories > capabilities > contracts along which the software is grown; read this to know what the tree is, where it lives, and what it is not.
body:
**In one line.** The work surface: the living, populated DAG of stories > capabilities > contracts along which the software is grown — authored as files under `stories/`, rendered by the storytree CLI and the studio, and watched by a human as the map of the AI-driven SDLC.

## What it is

One of the three working surfaces — the **tree** is the work, the noticeboard is the sessions, the library is the knowledge. The story tree is the project's **research object** (ADR-0030): the map of the AI-SDLC a human maintains observability over — not an incidental task list the agents happen to keep. Concretely today it is authored **file-per-unit** as YAML-frontmatter markdown under `stories/`: a `story.md` per story plus one file per capability (contracts live inline in their capability file by the seed convention; a registered-buildable contract such as `verdict-line` gets its own file), with lifecycle statuses and `depends_on` edges carried in the frontmatter. The storytree CLI walks it (`story build` chains a story's nodes in dependency order) and the studio renders it. A node **exists** the moment it is authored; green-ness is per-node and earned later, through the prove-it-gate. This term names the *surface*; for the tiers that populate it, see **story**, **capability**, **contract** (ADR-0002).

## What it is not

Not the **git commit tree** — git holds the code and a generated markdown view; the story tree is the work map above it. Not the bare **DAG** — that term names the *shape*; the story tree is the populated, living artifact that has that shape. Not the **library** — that surface holds the knowledge; the tree holds the work.
provenance: Premise reframed by ADR-0030 — the story tree is the research object; the working-surface trio completed by the noticeboard (ADR-0033, proposed).

title: Template — process
description: Fillable scaffold for a new process artifact (a repeatable operating ceremony).
body:
**The ceremony.** _What this process accomplishes, in one sentence._

## Trigger

_The moment a session runs this — the observable condition, not a vibe._

## Steps

_The ordered ceremony, one numbered step per action — each step names the command it runs or the surface it touches._

## Surfaces

_Which surfaces this touches — tree, noticeboard, library, repo/CI — and what it reads or writes on each._

## Failure modes

_What breaks when the ceremony is skipped or a step runs out of order — concrete incidents over hypotheticals._

## Verification

_What deterministically checks the ceremony was followed — a gate, a CI job, a test. If nothing checks it, say so explicitly._
